results.possibleIdentity:
Myron Greenbaum was a prominent electrical engineer based in New York and New Jersey, active from the mid-1960s through the early 2000s. He earned a Master of Science in Electrical Engineering (MSEE) from New York University and became a Licensed Professional Engineer (PE) in New York. Throughout much of his career, Greenbaum was associated with ITT Avionics (located in Nutley and Clifton, New Jersey), where he eventually served as the Director of Advanced Systems. His technical expertise spanned a wide range of fields including electrical engineering, radar systems, aeronautics/aerospace, sonar, circuit design, microwaves, and system effectiveness. He was particularly involved in the development of advanced electronic warfare (EW) systems and modeling and simulation methodologies. 

Greenbaum held significant leadership positions within the technical community, notably serving as the President of the IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Society (AESS) from 1998 to 1999 and as a member of the IEEE Sensors Council. He lived in a Brooklyn Heights brownstone during the early years of his marriage (married in 1965) before moving to South Orange, New Jersey. He is likely the Myron Greenbaum associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as his location in Brooklyn and Nutley and his work at ITT during the late 1960s and 1970s aligned with the organization's recruitment of engineers to collaborate with artists on technical projects. Search results also distinguish him as a real-life engineer, noting that he is distinct from a fictional 'Dr. Myron Greenbaum' cited in the book 'Go Ask Alice'.

webSearchQueries: Myron Greenbaum "Experiments in Art and Technology"
